coredata.dc:title: Influence of chemical states of doped nitrogen on photoluminescence intensity of hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Niino, Shinichiro
Takeshita, Satoru
Iso, Yoshiki
Isobe, Tetsuhiko
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-containing carbon dots were synthesized from glucose and glucosamine by hydrothermal treatment. The relation between the PL properties of the carbon dots and chemical states of nitrogen in them are discussed. The actual N/C ratio of synthesized samples increased from 0.00 to 0.08 as the nominal molar percentage of glucosamine/(glucose+glucosamine), x N, was increased from 0 to 100mol%. The samples were nanoparticles with microscopic local graphite structure. Some nitrogen atoms were doped in the graphite skeleton, and others were present as surface NH2 groups. The nanoparticles exhibited blue photoluminescence (PL) under near-ultraviolet excitation. The PL peak shifted to longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased. PL intensity was proportional to the absorbance of the carbon dots, and both increased with the amount of nitrogen doped in the graphite skeleton. Other carbon dots were synthesized using ammonium hydroxide as a nitrogen source instead of glucosamine. These samples possessed abundant surface NH2 groups, and exhibited no change in PL intensity with increasing NH2 content. Nitrogen doped in the graphite skeleton formed donor levels in the energy band of the carbon dots, increasing the amount of absorption centers and enhancing PL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Nitrogen
Photoluminescence
Nanoparticle
Hydrothermal synthesis
Glucose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structures of (a) glucose and (b) glucosamine.
Changes in actual N/C ratios of (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A as functions of x N and x A, respectively.
(a–c) TEM images and (d–f) corresponding size distributions of CD-N prepared with different molecular percentages of glucosamine x N: (a, d) 0, (b, e) 10, and (c, f) 100mol%.
(a–c) TEM images and (d–f) corresponding size distributions of CD-A prepared with different amounts of aqueous NH3 x A: (a, d) 0, (b, e) 67, and (c, f) 134μL.
Changes in the mean particle size and its standard deviation for CD-N and CD-A with N/C ratio.
Raman spectra of powder samples (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A. In (a), x N : (i) 0, (ii) 5, (iii) 10, (iv) 50, (v) 75, and (vi) 100mol%. In (b), x A: (i) 0, (ii) 67, (iii) 101, and (iv) 134μL.

FT-IR spectra of powder samples (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A. In (a), x N: (i) 0, (ii) 5, (iii) 10, (iv) 50, (v) 75, and (vi) 100mol%. In (b), x A: (i) 0, (ii) 67, (iii) 101, and (iv) 134μL. FT-IR spectra of glucose and glucosamine starting materials are also shown.
N 1s spectra of powder samples of (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A. In (a), x N: (i) 5, (ii) 10, (iii) 50, (iv) 75, and (v) 100mol%. In (b), x A: (i) 67, (ii) 101, and (iii) 134μL.
Changes in y N–C and y N–H for powder samples (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A as functions of x N and x A, respectively.

UV–visible absorption spectra of colloidal solutions of (a) CD-N and (b) CD-A (0.01gL−1). In (a), x N: (i) 0, (ii) 5, (iii) 10, (iv) 50, (v) 75, and (vi) 100mol%. In (b), x A: (i) 0, (ii) 67, (iii) 101 and (iv) 134μL.

(i) PLE and (ii–vii) PL spectra of CD-N dispersions (0.01gL−1) prepared with x N of (a) 0 and (b) 50mol%. λ em: (i) 464nm. λ ex: (ii) 340nm, (iii) 350nm, (iv) 360nm, (v) 370nm, (vi) 380nm, and (vii) 390nm.

(i) PLE and (ii–vii) PL spectra of CD-A dispersions (0.01gL−1) prepared with x A of (a) 0 and (b) 67μL. λ em: (i) 466nm. λ ex: (ii) 340nm, (iii) 350nm, (iv) 360nm, (v) 370nm, (vi) 380nm, and (vii) 390nm.
Changes in (a) maximum PL intensity and (b) absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength of CD-N and CD-A with N/C ratio.
Change of maximum PL intensity of CD-N and CD-A with absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength.
Changes of the maximum PL intensity of CD-N and CD-A as functions of (a) y N–C and (b) y N–H.
Schematic energy diagrams of carbon dots without (left) and with (right) nitrogen.

head.ce:title: Influence of chemical states of doped nitrogen on photoluminescence intensity of hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University, 3-14-1 Hiyoshi, Kohoku-Ku, Yokohama 223-8522, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Keio University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Research Institute for Chemical Process Technology, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Tsukuba Central 5, 1-1-1 Higashi, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-8565, Japan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-containing carbon dots were synthesized from glucose and glucosamine by hydrothermal treatment. The relation between the PL properties of the carbon dots and chemical states of nitrogen in them are discussed. The actual N/C ratio of synthesized samples increased from 0.00 to 0.08 as the nominal molar percentage of glucosamine/(glucose+glucosamine), x N, was increased from 0 to 100mol%. The samples were nanoparticles with microscopic local graphite structure. Some nitrogen atoms were doped in the graphite skeleton, and others were present as surface NH2 groups. The nanoparticles exhibited blue photoluminescence (PL) under near-ultraviolet excitation. The PL peak shifted to longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased. PL intensity was proportional to the absorbance of the carbon dots, and both increased with the amount of nitrogen doped in the graphite skeleton. Other carbon dots were synthesized using ammonium hydroxide as a nitrogen source instead of glucosamine. These samples possessed abundant surface NH2 groups, and exhibited no change in PL intensity with increasing NH2 content. Nitrogen doped in the graphite skeleton formed donor levels in the energy band of the carbon dots, increasing the amount of absorption centers and enhancing PL.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are nanoparticles that are mainly composed of carbon. Although a clear definition of carbon dots has not been established, they generally consist of a mixture of sp2 and sp3 carbon species, and may contain several kinds of functional groups [1]. The outstanding properties of fluorescent carbon materials, such as physiological stability, pH sensitivity, and low toxicity, make them attractive for use in energy-related devices, composite materials, and environmental and biological applications. For example, the generation and transport of electrons in photoexcited carbon dots have been exploited in solar cells [2–4], organic light-emitting diodes [5], photodetectors [6], and photocatalysts [7]. The photoluminescence (PL) quenching of carbon dots has been used in sensors that detect biomolecules [8,9], metal ions [10–15], and toxic or dangerous substances [16,17]. The PL stability and low toxicity of carbon dots are important factors in bioimaging [18,19], biosensors [20], drug delivery [21], and medical diagnosis [22].

The synthesis methods of carbon dots can be divided into top-down methods and bottom-up molecular approaches. Top-down methods include decreasing the size of various carbon sources such as graphite [23–25], graphene oxide [26], carbon fiber [7,27], and carbon nanotubes [28,29] by arc discharge [30,31], laser ablation [32], electrochemical oxidation [33,34] or chemical oxidation using concentrated oxidizing acid (HNO3 or a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3) [35]. Carbon dots made by top-down methods possess a perfect sp2 carbon structure. Meanwhile, bottom-up molecular approaches include carbonization [36] or dehydration [37–39] of carbohydrates, self-assembly of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [40], and organic synthesis from small molecules [41]. In particular, there are numerous approaches for performing the carbonization and dehydration processes, such as hydrothermal [42–44], microwave [45,46] and combustion [47] methods, pyrolysis in concentrated oxidizing acid [48], and carbonization in a microreactor [49]. Carbon dots prepared by bottom-up approaches contain a mixture of sp2 and sp3 carbons. They also possess –OH, –COOH, and –C=O groups.

Despite the diversity of their structures, carbon dots display several similar optical features including strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region (230–320nm) and dependence of optical properties on pH and solvent [50]. One of the most attractive properties of carbon dots is that their PL wavelength depends on excitation wavelength. This dependence possibly originates from their variety of surface functional groups [41,51]. In addition, several researchers have reported that doping carbon dots with nitrogen increases their PL intensity [40,52], although the details on chemical states of doped nitrogen contributing to PL enhancement has not been examined yet.

In the present study, we investigate the effect of different chemical states of doped nitrogen on the PL intensity of carbon dots. We prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots by the hydrothermal method using glucose as a carbon source. Glucosamine resembles glucose except that an NH2 group substitutes one OH group, as shown in Fig. 1. By using mixtures of glucose and glucosamine with different ratios, we prepare nitrogen-containing carbon dot samples with various nitrogen concentrations. We also prepare carbon dots with abundant NH2 groups by treatment with ammonium hydroxide instead of glucosamine. We examine the relation between the PL properties of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the chemical states of nitrogen within them.

A total amount of 2.22mmol of D(+)-glucose (Wako Pure Chemical, 98.0%) and D(+)-glucosamine hydrochloride (Kanto Chemical, 98.0%) were dissolved in ultrapure water (40.0mL). The nominal molar percentage of glucosamine/(glucose + glucosamine), x N, was varied in the range of 0≤x N≤100mol%. The pH of this solution was adjusted to 4.3 by adding potassium dihydrogen phosphate (Wako Pure Chemical, 99.5%). The solution was poured into a Teflon-vessel and purged with Ar gas for 30min at a flow rate of 300mLmin−1. The vessel was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave (Berghof, DAB-2, inner volume 50mL). The autoclave was sealed, heated to 250°C, and kept at that temperature for 30min. After cooling to room temperature, large particles of by-products were removed by centrifugation at a centrifugal acceleration of ~9100g (9000rpm using a rotor with a diameter of 10cm) for 15min. The supernatant was dialyzed in ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off 3500Da) until its conductivity decreased to ~3mSm−1. The dispersion was passed through a membrane filter (pore size 0.2μm) to yield a colloidal solution of carbon dots. A powder sample was obtained by freeze-drying the colloidal solution for 24h. Each sample was prepared five times to determine the experimental deviation. The samples prepared in this way are named CD-N.

The colloidal solution (20mL) of CD-N prepared with x N=0 was concentrated to 10mL by evaporation. The solution was poured into a Teflon-vessel and purged with Ar gas. The vessel was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave and purged with Ar gas. Aqueous NH3 (Wako Pure Chemical, 28wt%) was added to the solution. The nominal amount of aqueous NH3, x A, was varied in the range of 0≤x A≤134μL. The autoclave was sealed, heated to 150°C, and kept at that temperature for 60min. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was purified by the above-mentioned process. The samples prepared in this manner are denoted CD-A.

Particle morphologies were observed with a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Tecnai 12). Samples for TEM observation were prepared by drying a drop of a concentrated aqueous carbon dot dispersion on a carbon-reinforced collodion film of a copper grid at 30°C. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles were measured on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Rint-2200) with a Cu Kα radiation source and monochromator. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman microscope (Renishaw, inVia StreamLine) with a 532-nm laser. The Raman peaks were decomposed by using a software (OriginLab Corp., Origin Pro 8). The chemical compositions of powder samples were measured using an elemental analyzer (Elementar Analytical, Vario EL). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra of pressed KBr disks containing samples were measured on a FT-IR spectrometer (JASCO, FT/IR-4200). Chemical states of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an XPS instrument (JEOL, JPS-9010TR) with a Mg Kα radiation source. Powder samples were pressed to form disks for XPS measurements. A portion of the disk surface was coated with Au using an ion sputtering device for charge-up correction (Hitachi, E-1030). Optical absorption spectra were measured on a UV–visible spectrometer (JASCO, V-570). The net absorbance of carbon dots was calculated by subtracting the absorbance of ultrapure water from that of a colloidal solution. PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO, FP-6500) using a 150-W Xe lamp as the excitation source. The quantum yields of colloidal solutions were measured relative to that (95%) of Rhodamine 6G in ethanol. Each spectral response was calibrated using an ethylene glycol solution of Rhodamine B (5.5gL−1) and a standard light source (JASCO, ESC-333). A concentration of carbon dot dispersion was adjusted to 0.01gL−1 for measurement of optical absorption, PL and PLE spectra. PL decay curves and PL lifetimes were measured on an PL lifetime spectrometer (Quantaurus-Tau, C11367-25, Hamamatsu Photonics).

Fig. 2 shows the changes in actual N/C ratios of CD-N and CD-A determined by elemental analysis as functions of x N and x A, respectively. The actual N/C ratio of CD-N increased from 0.00 to 0.08 as x N increased from 0 to 100mol%. The actual N/C ratio of CD-A increased from 0.00 to 0.06 as x A increased from 0 to 134μL.

Fig. 3 displays TEM images and size distributions of CD-N prepared with different molecular percentages of glucosamine (i.e., different x N). TEM images and size distributions of CD-A prepared with different amounts of aqueous NH3 (i.e., different x A) are shown in Fig. 4. All of the samples are composed of nanoparticles irrespective of x N or x A.

The mean particle size and its standard deviation for CD-N and CD-A were measured from the TEM images, and are plotted as a function of the actual N/C ratio in Fig. 5. The mean size of CD-N slightly decreased from ~8 to ~6nm as the N/C ratio increased from 0 to 0.08. This decrease in mean size is possibly caused by a decrease in the condensation rate, which is influenced by the NH2 groups of glucosamine, with increasing N/C ratio. In contrast, the mean size of CD-A was constant regardless of N/C ratio.

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of powder samples of CD-N and CD-A. Two broad bands with maxima at ~1300 and ~1550cm−1 were observed for all of the samples. The band at ~1300cm−1 is assigned to the D band, which corresponds to the sp3-related defects of graphite [53]. The band at ~1550cm−1 is consistent with the G band, which corresponds to the lattice vibration of graphite [53]. The broad G band indicates low crystallinity of the graphite structure of the carbon dots. The intensity ratio of decomposed D band relative to decomposed G band has no clear relationship with amounts of nitrogen source, x N and x A (see “Raman spectrum analysis” in Supplementary material).

FT-IR spectra of powder samples of CD-N, CD-A, glucose, and glucosamine are illustrated in Fig. 7. As shown in Fig. 7a, characteristic sharp peaks of glucose in the 500–1500cm−1 range became broad after hydrothermal treatment, indicating the decomposition of glucose and glucosamine. The peaks at 1620 and 970cm−1 (peaks 2 and 5, respectively) are assigned to the symmetric stretching and deformation vibrations of C=C bonds in the graphite structure [54]. Peak 2 is also attributed to the bending vibration of O–H [54]. A strong peak at 1700cm−1 (peak 1) is assigned to the symmetric stretching vibration of C=O bonds [54]. Two broad absorption bands at ~1400 and ~1120cm−1 (peaks 3 and 4) are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C bonds, respectively [54]. Moreover, a peak at 800cm−1 (peak 6) corresponding to the wagging vibration of NH2 groups was observed for CD-N prepared with x N = 10, 50, 75, and 100mol% [55–57]. This peak was also observed for CD-A prepared with x A=67, 101, and 134μL, as depicted in Fig. 7b. This peak confirms the presence of NH2 groups on the particles. According to the results of Raman and FT-IR spectroscopies, each sample consists of particles with graphite structure containing C=O, C–O–C, O–H, and N–H bonds.

XRD patterns of the samples (Figs. S2 and S3) contained no diffraction peaks originating from crystalline graphite irrespective of x N and x A. Judging from these results, the samples only contain microscopic local areas of graphite structure that are too small to detect by XRD.

The presence of C, H, O, and N in the samples was confirmed by XPS. Decomposed XPS data for the C 1s peak of powder samples of CD-N and CD-A are presented Figs. S4 and S5, respectively. Each C 1s peak is composed of three peaks: peak 1 at 288.4eV corresponding to C=O bonds, peak 2 at 286.2eV corresponding to C–O bonds, and peak 3 at 284.8eV corresponding to C–C and C=C bonds [37]. These components are attributed to the graphite structure with C=O and C–O–C bonds, as confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy.

Fig. 8 shows the decomposed N 1s spectra for powder samples of CD-N and CD-A. Each N 1s peak is composed of two peaks: peak 1 at 402.0eV corresponding to N–C bonds in graphite structure, and peak 2 at 400.5eV corresponding to N–H bonds of NH2 groups [52]. These results indicate that some nitrogen atoms were doped in the graphite skeleton, and other nitrogen atoms were present as NH2 groups on the particle surface.

The proportions of N–C and N–H bonds were calculated from decomposed N 1s XPS peak areas. Actual amounts of N–C bonds y N–C and N–H bonds y N–H were calculated by multiplication of the actual N/C ratio by the proportions of N–C and N–H bonds, respectively. Changes in y N–C and y N–H for powder samples of CD-N and CD-A are plotted as functions of x N and x A, respectively, in Fig. 9. The y N–C value of CD-N increased from 0.00 to 0.06 as x N increased from 0 to 100mol%. In contrast, y N–H of CD-N increased from 0.00 to 0.04 up to x N=50mol%, and then decreased to 0.02. Meanwhile, y N–C and y N–H of CD-A increased monotonically from 0.00 to 0.02 and 0.00 to 0.05, respectively, with increasing x A. The y N–H value was larger than that of y N–C, showing that N–H bonds were preferentially formed over N–C ones for CD-A.

Fig. 10 shows UV–visible absorption spectra of colloidal solutions of CD-N and CD-A. An absorption shoulder at 228nm (peak 1) is assigned to the π→π* transition of C=C bonds, which is similar to that of graphene quantum dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment [43,58,59]. The absorption peak at 282nm (peak 2) is assigned to the n→π* transition of C=O bonds [50].

As illustrated in Figs. S6 and S7, colloidal solutions of CD-N and CD-A, respectively, emit blue PL under near-UV excitation. Fig. 11 shows PL and PLE spectra of colloidal solutions of CD-N with x N of (a) 0 and (b) 50mol%. Fig. 12 shows the PL and PLE spectra of colloidal solutions of CD-A prepared with x A of (a) 0 and (b) 67μL. A broad excitation band with its maximum at 366nm was observed in the PLE spectra. Meanwhile, the PL spectra contained broad emission bands with maxima at 440–470nm. The emission peak shifted to longer wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is characteristic of luminescent carbon dots [60]. Although the detailed mechanism of the excitation wavelength dependence of carbon dots has not been clarified, several researchers have proposed that the excitation wavelength dependence of PL spectra originates from the particle size distribution and/or variety of surface functional groups [51,61].

Fig. 13a shows the changes in maximum PL intensity of CD-N and CD-A monitored at the optimum excitation wavelength with N/C ratio. The PL intensity of CD-N increased with N/C ratio. This result is consistent with the previous research showing that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots increased when they contained nitrogen [52]. In contrast, the N/C ratio has no clear relation with PL intensity for CD-A. We also note that the PL intensity of CD-A was lower than that of CD-N. Fig. 13b shows the change in absorbance of CD-N and CD-A monitored at the optimum excitation wavelength with N/C ratio. The absorbance increased with increasing N/C ratio for both CD-N and CD-A.

The PL maximum intensities of CD-N and CD-A were also plotted as a function of absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 14. The PL intensity is proportional to the absorbance for CD-N, while CD-A exhibit no clear relation between the PL intensity and absorbance. Fig. S8 depicts the change in quantum yield of CD-N and CD-A monitored at the optimum excitation wavelength with N/C ratio. The quantum yield of CD-N remained constant regardless of N/C ratio. This result is consistent with the observed proportional relationship between the PL intensity and absorbance of CD-N. In contrast, the quantum yield of CD-A gradually decreased from 0.9 to 0.5 with increasing N/C ratio. The quantum yields of the prepared samples of CD-N and CD-A were lower than the reported values (7–11%) of glucose-derived carbon dots [58]. PL lifetimes calculated from PL decay curves (Figs. S9 and S10) were shorter than previous-reported values for nitrogen-doped carbon dots [62,63]. This indicates that our samples have comparatively many defects causing quenching through non-radiative relaxation processes.

Fig. 15 illustrates the change of maximum PL intensity of CD-N and CD-A as functions of y N–C and y N–H. The PL intensity of CD-N increased with y N–C, but did not show obvious relationship with y N–H. This result reveals that the increase of PL intensity can be mainly attributed to the increase in the amount of N–C bonds in CD-N. This suggests that doping of nitrogen into the graphite skeleton can effectively increase the PL intensity of carbon dots. Doping the graphite skeleton with nitrogen atoms forms donor levels in the energy band of the carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 16. This in turn increases the amount of absorption centers, contributing to PL enhancement. The PL intensity of CD-A remains constant as y N–H changes. In contrast, doping carbon dots with nitrogen through the inclusion of NH2 groups produces several surface trap levels, resulting in a decrease in PL intensity, as also shown in Fig. 16. This effect caused the PL intensity of CD-A to decrease with increasing y N–C.

Nitrogen-containing carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using glucose as a carbon source and glucosamine as a source of carbon and nitrogen. The actual N/C ratio increased from 0.00 to 0.08 as the nominal molar percentage of glucosamine was increased from 0 to 100mol%. The PL intensity of the nitrogen-doped carbons dots increased with the amount of N–C bonds. This increase of PL intensity is attributed to the increase of absorbance of the carbon dots with rising content of N–C bonds. The increase of absorbance is probably caused by the formation of donor levels corresponding to the N–C bonds in the graphite skeleton. In addition, carbon dots with abundant NH2 groups were synthesized by treatment of carbon dots without any nitrogen in aqueous NH3. However, these carbon dots exhibited no change in PL intensity with increasing content of NH2 groups. These results also indicate that doping of carbon dots with nitrogen atoms as NH2 groups produces several surface trap levels, resulting in a decrease of PL intensity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Hamamatsu Photonics for measurement of PL decay curves and PL lifetimes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.08.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
